title: Pre-Calculus: identity reference and graded questions
description: A compact reference of the core algebraic and trig identities (rule plus a one-line note), followed by graded pre-calculus questions. Notes are kept short and plainly worded for a dyslexic reader.
subject: Pre-Calculus

identities.note: Dividing by a fraction flips it, then multiply.
Common bottom first, then add tops.
The power spreads over a product.
Negative power means reciprocal; anything to the 0 is 1.
A fraction power is a root.
Line through a point with known slope.
Composition: do the inner rule first.

identities.statement: \text{Inverse: swap } x \text{ and } y,\ \text{then solve for } y

identities.note: Solves any quadratic ax^2 + bx + c = 0.
Log of a product is a sum.
Log of a quotient is a difference.
A power comes down to the front.
Base to itself is 1; log of 1 is 0.
Three forms of the cosine double angle.

questions.question: Show that $f(x) = x^3 - x$ is an odd function.
questions.solution: Replace $x$ with $-x$: $f(-x) = (-x)^3 - (-x) = -x^3 + x = -(x^3 - x) = -f(x)$. Since $f(-x) = -f(x)$, the function is odd.

questions.question: Describe how the graph of $y = (x - 2)^2 + 3$ comes from $y = x^2$.
questions.solution: The $(x - 2)$ moves the graph right by $2$. The $+3$ moves it up by $3$. So the vertex goes from $(0,0)$ to $(2, 3)$.

questions.question: Given $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ and $g(x) = x^2 + 1$, find $(f \circ g)(x)$ and its domain.
questions.answer: $(f \circ g)(x) = \sqrt{x^2 + 1}$; domain is all real numbers.
questions.solution: $(f \circ g)(x) = f(x^2 + 1) = \sqrt{x^2 + 1}$. Since $x^2 + 1$ is always at least $1$, the square root is always defined. So the domain is every real number.

questions.question: Find the range of $f(x) = x^2 + 1$.
questions.solution: The smallest value of $x^2$ is $0$, at $x = 0$. So the smallest output is $0 + 1 = 1$. The range is $y \geq 1$.

questions.question: Find the domain of $f(x) = \dfrac{x + 1}{x^2 - x - 6}$.
questions.solution: The bottom cannot be zero. Factor it: $x^2 - x - 6 = (x - 3)(x + 2)$. This is zero at $x = 3$ and $x = -2$, so those are not allowed.

questions.question: Solve $|2x - 1| < 5$.
questions.solution: An absolute value less than $5$ means the inside is between $-5$ and $5$: $-5 < 2x - 1 < 5$. Add $1$: $-4 < 2x < 6$. Divide by $2$: $-2 < x < 3$.

questions.question: Solve $|x + 3| \geq 2$.
questions.solution: An absolute value at least $2$ means the inside is $\geq 2$ or $\leq -2$. So $x + 3 \geq 2$ gives $x \geq -1$, and $x + 3 \leq -2$ gives $x \leq -5$.

questions.question: Solve the system $\begin{cases} 2x + y = 7 \\ x - y = 2 \end{cases}$
questions.solution: Add the two equations to remove $y$: $3x = 9$, so $x = 3$. Put this into $x - y = 2$: $3 - y = 2$, so $y = 1$.

questions.question: Solve the system $\begin{cases} y = x^2 \\ y = x + 2 \end{cases}$
questions.solution: Set the two right sides equal: $x^2 = x + 2$, so $x^2 - x - 2 = 0$. Factor: $(x - 2)(x + 1) = 0$, giving $x = 2$ or $x = -1$. Then $y = x^2$ gives $(2, 4)$ and $(-1, 1)$.

questions.question: Find the line perpendicular to $y = 2x + 1$ that passes through $(4, 3)$.
questions.solution: Perpendicular slopes multiply to $-1$, so the new slope is $-\dfrac{1}{2}$. Use the point: $y - 3 = -\dfrac{1}{2}(x - 4)$, which simplifies to $y = -\dfrac{1}{2}x + 5$.

questions.question: Factor $x^4 - 16$ completely.
questions.solution: This is a difference of squares: $x^4 - 16 = (x^2 - 4)(x^2 + 4)$. The first part factors again: $x^2 - 4 = (x - 2)(x + 2)$. The part $x^2 + 4$ does not factor with real numbers.

questions.question: Factor $x^3 - 4x$ completely.
questions.solution: Take out the common $x$: $x(x^2 - 4)$. Then $x^2 - 4$ is a difference of squares: $(x - 2)(x + 2)$. So the answer is $x(x - 2)(x + 2)$.

questions.question: Simplify $\dfrac{2x^2 + 5x - 3}{x + 3}$.
questions.solution: Factor the top: $2x^2 + 5x - 3 = (2x - 1)(x + 3)$. Cancel the common $(x + 3)$ to get $2x - 1$. This is valid as long as $x \neq -3$.

questions.question: Simplify $\dfrac{\frac{1}{x} - \frac{1}{3}}{x - 3}$.
questions.solution: Combine the top over a common bottom: $\dfrac{1}{x} - \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{3 - x}{3x}$. Now divide by $(x - 3)$, which flips it: $\dfrac{3 - x}{3x} \times \dfrac{1}{x - 3}$. Since $3 - x = -(x - 3)$, this becomes $-\dfrac{1}{3x}$.

questions.question: Write $x^2 + 6x + 5$ in the form $(x + p)^2 + q$, and give the vertex.
questions.solution: Take half of $6$, which is $3$, and square it to get $9$. Write $x^2 + 6x + 9 - 9 + 5 = (x + 3)^2 - 4$. The vertex is $(-3, -4)$.

questions.question: How many real solutions does $x^2 + 2x + 5 = 0$ have?
questions.solution: Check the discriminant $b^2 - 4ac = 2^2 - 4(1)(5) = 4 - 20 = -16$. It is negative, so there are no real solutions.

questions.question: Solve $5^x = \dfrac{1}{25}$.
questions.solution: Write $\dfrac{1}{25}$ as a power of $5$: $\dfrac{1}{25} = 5^{-2}$. So $5^x = 5^{-2}$, which means $x = -2$.

questions.question: Write $2\log x + \log y$ as a single logarithm.
questions.solution: The $2$ in front becomes a power: $2\log x = \log x^2$. Then a sum of logs is the log of a product: $\log x^2 + \log y = \log(x^2 y)$.

questions.question: Solve $\log(x) + \log(x - 3) = 1$ (base $10$).
questions.solution: Combine: $\log\big(x(x - 3)\big) = 1$, so $x(x - 3) = 10^1 = 10$. Then $x^2 - 3x - 10 = 0$, which factors to $(x - 5)(x + 2) = 0$. The logs need $x > 3$, so keep $x = 5$ and reject $x = -2$.

questions.question: Convert $120^\circ$ to radians.
questions.solution: Multiply by $\dfrac{\pi}{180}$: $120 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = \dfrac{120\pi}{180} = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$.

questions.question: Solve $\cos x = -\dfrac{1}{2}$ for $x$ in $[0, 2\pi)$.
questions.solution: Cosine is negative in the second and third quadrants. The reference angle is $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$, so $x = \pi - \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ and $x = \pi + \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{4\pi}{3}$.

questions.question: Solve $2\cos^2 x - 1 = 0$ for $x$ in $[0, 2\pi)$.
questions.solution: Rearrange: $\cos^2 x = \dfrac{1}{2}$, so $\cos x = \pm\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. The angles with this cosine are $\dfrac{\pi}{4}, \dfrac{3\pi}{4}, \dfrac{5\pi}{4}, \dfrac{7\pi}{4}$.

questions.question: Solve $\sin 2x = \sin x$ for $x$ in $[0, 2\pi)$.
questions.solution: Use $\sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x$: $2\sin x\cos x - \sin x = 0$, so $\sin x(2\cos x - 1) = 0$. Then $\sin x = 0$ gives $x = 0, \pi$, and $\cos x = \dfrac{1}{2}$ gives $x = \dfrac{\pi}{3}, \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$.

questions.question: Find the amplitude and period of $y = 3\sin(2x)$.
questions.solution: The number in front, $3$, is the amplitude. The period is $\dfrac{2\pi}{2} = \pi$, because the $2$ inside speeds up the wave.

questions.question: Find the 10th term of the sequence $3, 7, 11, 15, \dots$
questions.solution: The common difference is $4$. The rule is $a_n = 3 + (n - 1)\cdot 4$. So $a_{10} = 3 + 9 \times 4 = 39$.

questions.question: Find the 6th term of the sequence $2, 6, 18, 54, \dots$
questions.solution: The common ratio is $3$. The rule is $a_n = 2 \cdot 3^{\,n-1}$. So $a_6 = 2 \cdot 3^5 = 2 \times 243 = 486$.

questions.question: For $f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & x < 0 \\ 2x + 1 & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$, find $f(-3)$ and $f(2)$.
questions.solution: For $x = -3$, use the top piece (since $-3 < 0$): $(-3)^2 = 9$. For $x = 2$, use the bottom piece (since $2 \geq 0$): $2 \times 2 + 1 = 5$.

questions.question: Find the inverse of $f(x) = \sqrt{x - 1} + 2$ (for $x \geq 1$).
questions.solution: Set $y = \sqrt{x - 1} + 2$. Take $2$ from both sides: $y - 2 = \sqrt{x - 1}$. Square both sides: $(y - 2)^2 = x - 1$, so $x = (y - 2)^2 + 1$. Thus $f^{-1}(x) = (x - 2)^2 + 1$.

questions.question: For $f(x) = x^2$, simplify the difference quotient $\dfrac{f(x + h) - f(x)}{h}$.
questions.solution: Work out the top: $(x + h)^2 - x^2 = x^2 + 2xh + h^2 - x^2 = 2xh + h^2$. Divide by $h$: $\dfrac{2xh + h^2}{h} = 2x + h$.

questions.question: Find the range of $f(x) = -x^2 + 4$.
questions.solution: The biggest value of $-x^2$ is $0$, at $x = 0$. So the largest output is $0 + 4 = 4$, and the graph goes down from there. The range is $y \leq 4$.

questions.question: Find the domain of $f(x) = \sqrt{9 - x^2}$.
questions.solution: The inside of a square root cannot be negative: $9 - x^2 \geq 0$, so $x^2 \leq 9$. This means $x$ is between $-3$ and $3$.

questions.question: Solve $x^2 + 2x - 8 \leq 0$.
questions.solution: Factor: $(x + 4)(x - 2) \leq 0$. A product is zero or negative between its roots. The roots are $-4$ and $2$, so $-4 \leq x \leq 2$.

questions.question: Solve $|3x + 2| > 4$.
questions.solution: An absolute value greater than $4$ means the inside is $> 4$ or $< -4$. From $3x + 2 > 4$: $x > \dfrac{2}{3}$. From $3x + 2 < -4$: $x < -2$.

questions.question: Find the $x$-intercept and $y$-intercept of $3x - 4y = 12$.
questions.solution: For the $x$-intercept, set $y = 0$: $3x = 12$, so $x = 4$. For the $y$-intercept, set $x = 0$: $-4y = 12$, so $y = -3$.

questions.question: Find the line parallel to $y = -3x + 2$ that passes through $(1, 5)$.
questions.solution: Parallel lines share the same slope, so the slope is $-3$. Use the point: $y - 5 = -3(x - 1)$, which simplifies to $y = -3x + 8$.

questions.question: Find the vertex of $y = 2x^2 - 8x + 5$.
questions.solution: The $x$ of the vertex is $\dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{8}{4} = 2$. Put $x = 2$ back in: $y = 2(4) - 8(2) + 5 = -3$. So the vertex is $(2, -3)$.

questions.question: Solve $3x^2 - 5x - 2 = 0$.
questions.solution: Factor: $(3x + 1)(x - 2) = 0$. Set each part to zero: $3x + 1 = 0$ gives $x = -\dfrac{1}{3}$, and $x - 2 = 0$ gives $x = 2$.

questions.question: Simplify $\dfrac{(x^3 y^2)(x^2 y^4)}{x y^3}$.
questions.solution: Multiply the top by adding exponents: $x^{3+2} y^{2+4} = x^5 y^6$. Then divide by subtracting: $x^{5-1} y^{6-3} = x^4 y^3$.

questions.question: Simplify $(16x^4)^{3/4}$.
questions.solution: Apply the power to each part. $16^{3/4} = (2^4)^{3/4} = 2^3 = 8$, and $(x^4)^{3/4} = x^3$. So the answer is $8x^3$.

questions.question: Rationalize the denominator of $\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{5}}$.
questions.solution: Multiply the top and bottom by $\sqrt{5}$: $\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{5}} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{5}} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{5}}{5}$.

questions.question: Expand $\log\!\left(\dfrac{x^3}{y}\right)$ into separate logarithms.
questions.solution: A quotient becomes a difference: $\log x^3 - \log y$. Then the power comes down to the front: $3\log x - \log y$.

questions.question: Solve $\ln(x) + \ln(x - 1) = \ln(6)$.
questions.solution: Combine the left side: $\ln\big(x(x - 1)\big) = \ln 6$, so $x(x - 1) = 6$. Then $x^2 - x - 6 = 0$, which factors to $(x - 3)(x + 2) = 0$. The logs need $x > 1$, so keep $x = 3$.

questions.question: Find the exact values of $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ and $\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$.
questions.solution: These are standard angles. $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ is $60^\circ$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ is $30^\circ$. Both $\sin 60^\circ$ and $\cos 30^\circ$ equal $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

questions.question: Show that $1 + \tan^2 x = \sec^2 x$.
questions.solution: Start from $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$. Divide every term by $\cos^2 x$: $\dfrac{\sin^2 x}{\cos^2 x} + 1 = \dfrac{1}{\cos^2 x}$. This is $\tan^2 x + 1 = \sec^2 x$.

questions.question: A right triangle has legs $5$ and $12$. Find the hypotenuse and the sine of the angle opposite the side of length $5$.
questions.solution: By the Pythagorean theorem, the hypotenuse is $\sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{169} = 13$. Sine is opposite over hypotenuse: $\dfrac{5}{13}$.
